author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
The Necessity and Advantage of Rebuke.
title: The Necessity and Advantage of Rebuke.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 7 [V.]—The Necessity and Advantage of Rebuke.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: To this we answer: Whoever you are that do not the commandments of God that are already known to you, and do not wish to be rebuked, you must be rebuked even for that very reason that you do not wish to be rebuked.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: For you do not wish that your faults should be pointed out to you; you do not wish that they should be touched, and that such a useful pain should be caused you that you may seek the Physician; you do not desire to be shown to yourself, that, when you see yourself to be deformed, you may wish for the Reformer, and may supplicate Him that you may not continue in that repulsiveness.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: For it is your fault that you are evil; and it is a greater fault to be unwilling to be rebuked because you are evil, as if faults should either be praised, or regarded with indifference so as neither to be praised nor blamed, or as if, indeed, the dread, or the shame, or the mortification of the rebuked man were of no avail, or were of any other avail in healthfully stimulating, except to cause that He who is good may be besought, and so out of evil men who are rebuked may make good men who may be praised.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: For what he who will not be rebuked desires to be done for him, when he says, “Pray for me rather,”—he must be rebuked for that very reason that he may himself also do for himself; because that mortification with which he is dissatisfied with himself when he feels the sting of rebuke, stirs him up to a desire for more earnest prayer, that, by God’s mercy, he may be aided by the increase of love, and cease to do things which are shameful and mortifying, and do things praiseworthy and gladdening.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: This is the benefit of rebuke that is wholesomely applied, sometimes with greater, sometimes with less severity, in accordance with the diversity of sins; and it is then wholesome when the supreme Physician looks.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: For it is of no profit unless when it makes a man repent of his sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: And who gives this but He who looked upon the Apostle Peter when he denied, and made him weep?

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Whence also the Apostle Paul, after he said that they were to be rebuked with moderation who thought otherwise, immediately added, “Lest perchance God give them repentance, to the acknowledging of the truth, and they recover themselves out of the snares of the devil.”
